questions: Locate the article 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction' by Cat Webling and identify the specific Harry Potter 'headcanon' series recommended within it that explores an alternate universe where Harry is raised by a loving adoptive family. Find the primary text of this recommended storyline (often associated with the tag 'The Dogfather'). Based on the text of this specific AU, answer the following: 1) What specific genre of cookbooks does the adoptive father collect to help Harry connect with his heritage? 2) What two specific hardware items does Harry use to construct a makeshift collar for the dog Padfoot? 3) Upon witnessing Padfoot's first transformation into a human, what specific 'reverse' mythological condition does Harry hypothesize the man might have?

Analyze the historical lineage of transformative works by identifying three specific precursors to modern fanfiction: a 14th-century epic poem, a 1913 novel that functions as a crossover of a single author's works, and the fan-written stories emerging from the 'first modern fandom' surrounding a Victorian detective. For the epic poem, identify the work and explain the 'self-insert' aspect of its narrative. For the 1913 novel, name the title, its author, and the original author whose characters were repurposed. For the detective fandom, provide the specific term used for these fan-created stories and describe the type of non-fictional figures frequently inserted into the plots.

Identify the title of the 1913 novel often cited as an early precursor to modern fanfiction, which functions as a 'crossover' sequel integrating characters from multiple Jane Austen works. Next, name the specific 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine noted for its high quality and contributions from the show's actual writers, including D.C. Fontana. Finally, provide the name of the U.S. legal doctrine (comprising four specific factors) that generally permits the existence of such non-commercial transformative works, and the specific slang phrase used by the community when an author removes fandom-specific details to publish a fanfic as original commercial fiction.

rounds.question: Locate the article 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction' by Cat Webling and identify the specific Harry Potter 'headcanon' series recommended within it that explores an alternate universe where Harry is raised by a loving adoptive family. Find the primary text of this recommended storyline (often associated with the tag 'The Dogfather'). Based on the text of this specific AU, answer the following: 1) What specific genre of cookbooks does the adoptive father collect to help Harry connect with his heritage? 2) What two specific hardware items does Harry use to construct a makeshift collar for the dog Padfoot? 3) Upon witnessing Padfoot's first transformation into a human, what specific 'reverse' mythological condition does Harry hypothesize the man might have?
checklist_width: Collar item 1: A length of curb chain (or chain)
checklist_depth: Target Story: 'The Dogfather' (or headcanons by nonasuch)
Logic: The user must link the recommendation in Webling's guide to the external story text to find the specific plot details not present in the guide itself.
rounds.rationale: This query utilizes Deep reasoning by requiring the agent to first analyze the 'Insider's Guide' to identify a specific, unnamed recommendation (the 'Dogfather' headcanons). It then applies Wide aggregation by forcing the agent to locate the external text of that story and retrieve three distinct, granular details (cookbooks, collar materials, dialogue) that are not present in the initial guide, ensuring the agent has found and read the correct primary source.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: 2 Feb 2018 all right. so. this is a Harry Potter AU, in rambly and abbreviated form. this is a version of events where, on the morning of November 1st, 1981, the police are ca
rounds.answer_a:
The Harry Potter alternate universe series "The Dogfather" by hctiB_notsoB, recommended in Cat Webling's Book Riot article "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction" [1], depicts Sirius Black raising Harry Potter as his adoptive son following his escape from Azkaban. To help Harry explore his maternal family heritage, Sirius collects Jewish cookbooks featuring traditional recipes and cultural dishes from Lily's background [2]. When young Harry encounters Padfoot and attempts to restrain the large dog, he improvises a makeshift collar using spare copper tubing and a hose clamp sourced from Sirius's garage workshop [2]. After observing Padfoot's unexpected transformation into Sirius Black, Harry hypothesizes the condition might be "reverse lycanthropy," theorizing the man voluntarily becomes a dog at will rather than transforming involuntarily during the full moon [3].

## References
[1] Webling, C. (2020). "An Insider's Guide to Fanfiction." *Book Riot*. https://bookriot.com/an-insiders-guide-to-fanfiction/
[2] hctiB_notsoB. (2014). *The Dogfather* series, early chapters. *Archive of Our Own*. https://archiveofourown.org/series/213742
[3] hctiB_notsoB. (2014). *The Dogfather* series, transformation scene. *Archive of Our Own*. https://archiveofourown.org/series/213742
rounds.answer_b:
The Harry Potter AU series recommended in Cat Webling’s article is the Dogfatherverse, with its primary text *The Dogfather* (hosted on AO3) [1]. The adoptive father collects magical British folk cookbooks to help Harry connect with his wizarding heritage [2]. To craft a makeshift collar for Padfoot, Harry uses a leather shoelace and a brass keyring [3]. When witnessing Padfoot’s first human transformation, Harry hypothesizes the man has a reverse werewolf condition—where a human shifts into a dog instead of the typical werewolf’s dog-to-human change [4]. This AU’s focus on Harry’s stable upbringing reconfigures his canon choices, aligning with the article’s spotlight on heartfelt alternate upbringings.

## References
[1] Webling, C. "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction." Book Riot, 15 Mar. 2022, https://bookriot.com/an-insiders-guide-to-fanfiction/.
[2] *The Dogfather*. Archive of Our Own, written by StarlightWriter, 18 Jul. 2019, https://archiveofourown.org/works/17892345.
[3] Dogfatherverse Fandom Database, Harry Potter Fanon Wiki, https://harrypotterfanon.fandom.com/wiki/Dogfatherverse.
[4] *The Dogfather*, Archive of Our Own, Chapter 5, https://archiveofourown.org/works/17892345/chapters/42215672.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed the Deep Logic check by failing to identify the correct author of the target series (hollimichele/nonasuch). Agent A cited 'hctiB_notsoB' and Agent B cited 'StarlightWriter'. Consequently, both agents failed the Wide Aggregation check, hallucinating the answers to the specific plot questions. Both provided incorrect answers for the cookbook genre (Ground Truth: South Asian; Agent A: Jewish; Agent B: British folk) and the collar materials (Ground Truth: Curb chain/carabiner; Agent A: Copper tubing; Agent B: Shoelace). Although Agent B correctly retrieved the specific phrase 'reverse werewolf' (matches Ground Truth), the overwhelming inaccuracy in the other two questions and the source metadata makes both responses equally unhelpful.

rounds.question: Analyze the historical lineage of transformative works by identifying three specific precursors to modern fanfiction: a 14th-century epic poem, a 1913 novel that functions as a crossover of a single author's works, and the fan-written stories emerging from the 'first modern fandom' surrounding a Victorian detective. For the epic poem, identify the work and explain the 'self-insert' aspect of its narrative. For the 1913 novel, name the title, its author, and the original author whose characters were repurposed. For the detective fandom, provide the specific term used for these fan-created stories and describe the type of non-fictional figures frequently inserted into the plots.
checklist_width: Epic Poem: Identifies the work as Dante Alighieri’s 'The Divine Comedy'.
Epic Poem Detail: Notes the author (Dante) acts as a self-insert protagonist on a journey.
1913 Novel: Identifies the title as 'Old Friends and New Fancies'.
1913 Novel Detail: Identifies the author as Sybil G. Brinton.
1913 Novel Detail: States it is based on the works of Jane Austen.
Detective Fandom: Identifies the specific term for the stories as 'pastiches'.
Detective Fandom Detail: Notes the inclusion of real people, such as the author (Conan Doyle) or politicians.
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies the 14th-century epic as The Divine Comedy based on the 'Bible retelling' logic.
Correctly identifies the 1913 novel as Old Friends and New Fancies based on the 'Jane Austen crossover' logic.
Correctly identifies the Victorian detective fandom as Sherlock Holmes and the output as 'pastiches'.
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by describing the entities via their historical context and literary characteristics (14th-century self-insert, 1913 crossover, Victorian detective fandom) rather than naming them. It requires wide aggregation by asking for specific metadata (author, title, terms) and narrative details (self-insert nature, real-world figure inclusion) that are scattered across the 'History of Fanfiction' section of the source text.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The 14th-century epic poem is *The Divine Comedy* (1308–1320) by Dante Alighieri, which features Dante himself as the first-person pilgrim protagonist journeying through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise—an explicit authorial self-insertion into the narrative structure[1].

The 1913 crossover novel is *The Man with Two Left Feet* by P.G. Wodehouse, a short story collection that bridges characters across his developing fictional universe, notably featuring Reggie Pepper—who appears in multiple Wodehouse series—thereby functioning as an early authorial crossover work[2].

Sherlock Holmes fandom produced "pastiches," fan-written stories that imitated Arthur Conan Doyle's style. These works frequently inserted historical non-fictional figures—including Oscar Wilde, Jack the Ripper, and British aristocracy—into Holmes's investigative adventures, blending factual personages with fictional detectives[3].

## References
[1] Dante Alighieri, *The Divine Comedy* (c. 1308–1320). The poet inserts himself as a character in his own theological epic.
[2] Wodehouse, P.G. *The Man with Two Left Feet* (1913). Collection demonstrates early character crossover within an author's oeuvre.
[3] Sherlock Holmes fandom studies. Term "pastiche" for fanfiction; historical figure insertion documented in early Holmesian apocrypha (1890s–1920s).
rounds.answer_b:
The 14th-century precursor is Dante Alighieri’s Divine Comedy: the poet inserts himself as the autobiographical protagonist, traversing Inferno, Purgatorio, and Paradiso to confront personal moral doubts and critique 14th-century Florentine political corruption and religious hypocrisy, using the self-insert to anchor abstract moral themes in personal experience[1]. The 1913 crossover novel is Anthony Hope’s Rupert of Henzau, which repurposes characters from his 1894 work The Prisoner of Zenda, extending the tale of royal impostor Rudolf Rassendyll and Princess Flavia’s forbidden romance amid court intrigue[2]. The Sherlock Holmes fandom (first modern fandom) produces fan stories termed “pastiches”[3]; these frequently include non-fictional Victorian figures like author Arthur Conan Doyle, prime ministers, or literary icons (e.g., Oscar Wilde) to blend fictional detective drama with real historical context and playful meta-commentary on the fandom’s relationship to the original texts[3].

## References
[1] Dante Society of America. (2024). The Divine Comedy’s Autobiographical Protagonist. Retrieved from https://dantesociety.org/autobiographical-narrative
[2] Oxford Reference. (2024). Rupert of Henzau (1913) by Anthony Hope. Retrieved from https://oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/acref/9780199571548.001.0001/acref-9780199571548-e-1852
[3] Sherlock Holmes Society of London. (2024). Holmes Pastiches and Historical Insertions. Retrieved from https://sherlockholmessociety.com/pastiche-historical-insertions
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed the primary 'Deep Logic' challenge of the query: identifying the specific 1913 novel that serves as a precursor to fanfiction. The correct answer is 'Old Friends and New Fancies' by Sybil G. Brinton (a crossover of Jane Austen's works). 

- Agent A incorrectly identified it as a P.G. Wodehouse collection (published 1917, not 1913).
- Agent B incorrectly identified it as 'Rupert of Henzau' (published 1898, and a direct sequel rather than a fan-created crossover).

While Agent A provided significantly better formatting (bullet points vs. Agent B's wall of text), Agent B was slightly more accurate on the Sherlock Holmes details (correctly noting the insertion of the author, Conan Doyle, as per the ground truth, whereas Agent A listed generic historical figures). Since both failed the central search entity retrieval, a Low Quality Tie is the most appropriate verdict.

rounds.question: Identify the title of the 1913 novel often cited as an early precursor to modern fanfiction, which functions as a 'crossover' sequel integrating characters from multiple Jane Austen works. Next, name the specific 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine noted for its high quality and contributions from the show's actual writers, including D.C. Fontana. Finally, provide the name of the U.S. legal doctrine (comprising four specific factors) that generally permits the existence of such non-commercial transformative works, and the specific slang phrase used by the community when an author removes fandom-specific details to publish a fanfic as original commercial fiction.
checklist_width: Identification of the 1913 novel: 'Old Friends and New Fancies'
Identification of the legal doctrine: 'Fair Use'
Identification of the slang phrase: 'Filing off the serial numbers'
checklist_depth: Entity: Old Friends and New Fancies (Logic: Matches 1913 publication date and Jane Austen crossover description)
Entity: Spockanalia (Logic: Matches 1967 date, Star Trek association, and D.C. Fontana/Roddenberry contribution)
Entity: Fair Use (Logic: Matches the 'four deciding factors' description regarding copyright)
Entity: Filing off the serial numbers (Logic: Matches the description of scrubbing identifying details for commercial publication)
rounds.rationale: The question requires 'Deep' reasoning to identify specific historical publications based on their descriptions (dates, authors, and content) without being given the titles. It requires 'Wide' aggregation by forcing the agent to synthesize information from the 'History of Fanfiction' section with the 'Fanfiction and Copyright' section to provide a complete answer regarding both literary origins and legal terminology.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The 1913 Jane Austen crossover sequel is *Old Friends and New Fancies* by Sybil G. Brinton, which integrates characters from *Pride and Prejudice*, *Sense and Sensibility*, *Emma*, and other novels into a unified narrative, effectively creating one of the earliest published fanfiction works[1]. The 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine recognized for its professional-quality content and contributions from series writers is *Spockanalia*, which featured scripts and stories from D.C. Fontana and other creators, setting a standard for fan-produced publications[2]. The U.S. legal doctrine that permits non-commercial transformative works is the **fair use doctrine**, which courts apply using four factors: (1) purpose and character of use, (2) nature of the copyrighted work, (3) amount and substantiality of the portion used, and (4) effect on the potential market for the original[3]. The slang phrase used when authors remove fandom-specific elements to publish fanfiction commercially is **"filing off the serial numbers,"** describing the process of obscuring a work's origins to create ostensibly original fiction for mainstream publication[4].

## References
[1] Brinton, Sybil G. *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913). Early published fanfiction combining multiple Jane Austen characters.
[2] *Spockanalia* (1967). Premier Star Trek fanzine featuring contributions from D.C. Fontana and professional writers.
[3] Copyright Act of 1976, 17 U.S.C. § 107. Establishes four-factor fair use test for transformative works.
[4] Fandom terminology. "Filing off the serial numbers" refers to removing identifiable fandom elements for commercial publication.
rounds.answer_b:
The 1913 novel often cited as an early precursor to modern fanfiction that crossovers multiple Jane Austen works is Mary L. Butler’s "Pride and Prejudice Continued: With a Crossover to Sense and Sensibility" [1]; it weaves Elizabeth Bennet (from Pride and Prejudice) and Elinor Dashwood (from Sense and Sensibility) into a new tale of rural English courtship, family strife, and hidden secrets, extending their arcs to explore unaddressed tensions between Austen’s distinct fictional worlds. The 1967 Star Trek fanzine renowned for its high quality and contributions from show writers like D.C. Fontana is Spockanalia [2]; it featured fan stories, critical essays, and artwork that deepened the show’s lore, bridging the gap between official creators and passionate fans during the show’s initial underrated television run. The U.S. legal doctrine permitting non-commercial transformative works is fair use [3], which evaluates four core factors: the purpose and character of the use, the nature of the original copyrighted work, the amount and substantiality of the portion used, and the effect of the use on the original work’s potential market value. The slang phrase for an author removing fandom-specific details to publish fanfic as original commercial fiction is “filing off the serial numbers” [4].

## References
[1] Austen Society of Britain. (2024). Mary L. Butler’s 1913 Austen Crossover Novel Analysis. Retrieved from https://austensociety.org/butler-1913-crossover
[2] Star Trek Fanarchive. (2024). Spockanalia (1967) Fanzine Legacy and Creator Contributions. Retrieved from https://startrekfanarchive.org/spockanalia-creator-links
[3] U.S. Copyright Office. (2024). Fair Use Doctrine: Four Factors Explained. Retrieved from https://copyright.gov/fairuse-factors-guide
[4] Fandom Studies Association. (2024). Fandom Slang Glossary: Commercialization and Adaptation Terms. Retrieved from https://fandomstudies.org/slang-commercial-adaptation
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A correctly identified all four entities requested in the prompt, specifically the 1913 novel *Old Friends and New Fancies* by Sybil G. Brinton. Agent B failed the first Deep Logic check by hallucinating a different author and title (Mary L. Butler) for the 1913 Austen crossover. Agent A also provided better formatting by bolding key terms for scannability.
